coredata.dc:title: Detection of HPV-16 L1 antigen using MoS2 nanosheets and amine-functionalized copper doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Swetha, Rajmohan
Divya, Karutha Pandian
A, Lakshmi Devi
Babu, T.G. Satheesh
coredata.dc:description: HPV-16L1 is one of the important biomarkers for chronic cervical cancer. The present work deals with the development of a label-free electrochemical immunosensor by modification of electrode surface using 2D MoS2 nanosheets and amine functionalized copper doped carbon dots (f-CuCD). The copper doped carbon dot with high surface area 2D material like MoS2 provides a stable platform for the immobilization of antibody which aids in the sensor performance. The use of MoS2/f-CuCD is the highlight of this work through which effective anti-HPV immobilization on the surface is achieved. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to analyse the response of different concentrations of HPV-16L1 on the modified screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE). On increasing the concentration of HPV-16L1, the RCT value gradually increased and the peak current decreased due to the low conductivity of the protein molecule. The developed immunosensor was found to detect wide concentration ranges of 0.01–100 ng/µL with a low detection limit of 8.30 pg/µL for HPV-16L1. Spiked human serum samples were also analysed. It also proves to be a sensitive, selective, stable and feasible tool for the determination of HPV-16L1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical
HPV-16 L1
Immunosensor
MoS2
Copper doped carbon dots
Label-free

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Cu doped amine functionalized carbon dots
Electrochemical behaviour of MoS2/SPCE and f-CuCD/SPCE towards HPV-16L1

R Swetha thanks Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham for providing financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD (A), Raman spectrum (B) and FTIR (C) of MoS2 nanoflowers.
UV- Vis (A) and FTIR (B) spectra of f-CuCD.
(A) TEM image of f-CuCD. (B,C) TEM images showing the dispersion of f-CuCD on MoS2 nanosheets.
CV (A) and EIS (B) response of bare SPCE, MoS2/SPCE, f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE, anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE and HPV-16L1/anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE.
(A) EIS response of modified electrode towards various concentrations of HPV-16L1 (0.01 ng/µL to 100 ng/µL). (B) Linear plot between logarithm of different HPV-16L1 concentrations and Rct.
(A,B): EIS response of HPV/anti-HPV/MoS2/SPCE (A) and HPV/anti-HPV/f-CuCD/SPCE (B).

(A) Selectivity of the developed immunosensor towards HPV-16L1, CA-125, HER-2 and IgG. (B) Reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor on five different electrodes. (C) Bar graph showing stability of anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE for 5 consecutive days. (D) CV graph showing the stability of anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE modified electrode for 5 consecutive days.
EIS response of human serum sample spiked with 0.1, 1, 10 and 50 ng/µL concentration of HPV-16L1.
Analytical performance of the f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE immunosensor along with the previously reported literature.

row.entry: Methylene blue labelled pencil graphite electrode/HPV probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test performance of the developed immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Detection of HPV-16 L1 antigen using MoS2 nanosheets and amine-functionalized copper doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amrita School of Physical Sciences Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Amrita School of Physical Sciences Coimbatore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bAmrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biomedical Engineering Research Centre, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cAmrita Biomedical Engineering Research Centre, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: HPV-16L1 is one of the important biomarkers for chronic cervical cancer. The present work deals with the development of a label-free electrochemical immunosensor by modification of electrode surface using 2D MoS2 nanosheets and amine functionalized copper doped carbon dots (f-CuCD). The copper doped carbon dot with high surface area 2D material like MoS2 provides a stable platform for the immobilization of antibody which aids in the sensor performance. The use of MoS2/f-CuCD is the highlight of this work through which effective anti-HPV immobilization on the surface is achieved. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to analyse the response of different concentrations of HPV-16L1 on the modified screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE). On increasing the concentration of HPV-16L1, the RCT value gradually increased and the peak current decreased due to the low conductivity of the protein molecule. The developed immunosensor was found to detect wide concentration ranges of 0.01–100 ng/µL with a low detection limit of 8.30 pg/µL for HPV-16L1. Spiked human serum samples were also analysed. It also proves to be a sensitive, selective, stable and feasible tool for the determination of HPV-16L1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cervical cancer has been reported as the fourth most common type of cancer occurring in women across the globe [1]. According to the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), there were about 5,57,088 cervical cancer cases reported in 2020 that led to 2,97,122 deaths [2]. Although vaccination is available, it is costly and accessible only to a small population around the world. Human papillomavirus (HPV) is one of the main causative agents for cervical cancer. HPV comprises of double-stranded non-enveloped DNA with more than 100 varieties of genotypes. These genotypes can be sub-divided depending on their risk factor as acute and chronic HPVs. Acute HPVs are mild and less harmful to the human body while chronic HPVs such as HPV-16L1 and HPV-18L1 prevail as the common causative agents of cervical cancer, causing about 93 to 100 % of invasive cervical cancer cases [3,4] Hence, detection of HPV-16L1 is important for early diagnosis to implement corresponding remedies. Techniques such as immunocytochemistry, immunogold agglutination and onco E6 test are used to detect HPV-16L1 biomarkers [5–7]. But these techniques suffer certain disadvantages due to their limited sensitivity, sophisticated operation and expensive equipment [8]. Thus, as an alternative, the electrochemical technique has been reported as an advantageous technique to offer rapid, sensitive, cost-effective and portable point-of-care devices towards the detection of several antigens/antibodies. In general, electrochemical immunosensor depends on the monitoring of signal arising as a result of the interaction between antibodies and antigens on the electrode surface. The performance of the immunosensor platform in detecting the signal is highly influenced by the electrode modification.

Screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) has been used to develop electrochemical immunosensors amenable to portability and miniaturization for point-of-care application. Screen printing microfabrication technique is used for the large scale and inexpensive production of disposable sensor strips [9]. In particular, the use of SPCE has gained interest due to their chemically inert nature, wide potential window and low background current [10].

Nanomaterials are particles in the diameter of less than 100 nm having contrast properties when compared with the bulk materials. They have shown varied applications in the field of sensors, dye removal, cytotoxic assay and examination of antimicrobial and anticancer activity [11–15]. The use of nanomaterials in the modification of electrode provides large surface area, binding capacity and bio-compatibility to load bio-recognition elements In addition, it also facilitates the electron transfer across the interface by providing more number of active sites [16]. Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) form one such class of nanomaterials with the general formula MX2 (M is a transition metal and X include chalcogenides like S,Se,Te). On exfoliating into single layers, TMDs show excellent properties like high surface-to-volume-ratio, tunable band gap, versatility in functionalization thereby making them useful in energy storage and sensing applications [17]. Among various TMDs, MoS2 is of high research interest in recent times mainly due to its semiconducting nature. MoS2 consists of a hexagonal plane of Mo atoms with hexagonal plane of S atoms on both sides [18].

Hui Ding et al., developed an electrochemical immunosensor using MoS2/NiCo heterostructure functionalized by in-situ grown palladium nanoparticles for sensitive detection of procalcitonin [19]. Hepatitis B surface antigen was detected using PtPd nanocubes@MoS2 nanoenzymes synthesized through in-situ polymerization by loading PtPd nanocubes on MoS2 nanosheets [20]. The polydopamine decorated MoS2 nanosheets used for the SARS-COV-2 nucleocapsid protein detection in clinical samples resulted in good agreement with the conventional RT-PCR test [21]. In the diagnosis of cancer biomarkers, MoS2 was widely utilized. A Metal organic framework (MOF) supported MoS2-AuNP (Cu-based MOFs (tricopper benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate, CuBTC) composite was used for the detection of ovarian cancer biomarker CA-125 [22]. Another work has been done using Au/MoS2/rGO nanocomposite for diagnosis of CA 27-29 biomarker [23]. Thus, MoS2 has been employed for the electrochemical immunosensing applications. MoS2 as a modification material for HPV-16L1 was also carried out in several literatures. However, due to the lateral movement of electrons towards the lamellar structure, the electron mobility is impeded and MoS2 nanosheets shows reduced conductivity [24]. Also, band gap of MoS2 increases with decrease in layer thickness below 100 nm [17]. Due to this, there is an intrinsic loss in conductivity which can be overcome by adding a conductive material. In recent years, carbon based nanomaterials are used to improve the conductivity of MoS2 based surfaces. A sensitive NO2 sensor was developed using 3D MoS2/rGO in which rGO provides enough surface area for MoS2 incorporation resulting in good analytical performance [25]. Woo Cho et al., prepared a mesoporous MoS2@carbon nanosphere for sensitive electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide. Thin layer of MoS2 was fabricated on the carbon nanospheres that helped in providing large surface area and pathway for ion transport [26]. The combination of MoS2 and carbon based materials have also been used in electrochemical immunosensor applications. Gourang Hari Gupta et al., fabricated an electrochemical immunosensor for detecting anti-mullerian hormone (AMH), a causative agent for poly-cystic ovarian syndrome using 2D MoS2/r-GO/PANI nanocomposite [27]. Ultrasensitive label free immunosensor using Au/MoS2/rGO for CA 27-29 detection has also been reported [28]. The preparation of L-cysteine functionalized MoS2@MWCNT nanocomposite has resulted in the sensitive detection of gentamicin due to the improvement in electron transfer efficiency [29]. The surface area and conductivity of the sensor surface has been improved using the combination of graphene nanoflakes and MWCNT along with MoS2. The anchoring of antibody was done using palladium nanoparticles as a linker. This sensor showed a LOD of 2 pg/mL for CEA detection [30]. Another label-free electrochemical immunosensor was developed utilizing rGO/MoS2/AuNPs nanocomposite for the detection of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) involving covalent immobilization of antibody [31]. From these literature, it is evident that the incorporation of carbon materials on MoS2 surface enhances the conductivity and electrocatalytic activity. This work aims in the incorporation of amine functionalized copper doped carbon dots (f-CuCD) on the MoS2 surface for enhanced sensing performance.

Carbon dots are zero dimensional material with excellent conductivity, flexibility, subdued environmental hazards, enhanced optical properties and stability when doped with heteroatoms [32]. The doping of CD with metal atoms especially transition metal ions instead of hetero atoms provide more number of active sites due to the presence of unoccupied orbitals and electrons which helps in the chelation of metal ion with the amino group present on the surface of CD [33]. Copper is widely used as a dopant since it exhibits low cytotoxicity and ease in interaction with the functional groups on CD surface [34]. Also, Carbon dots can be functionalized easily. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) has been used as a carbon source as well as functionalizing agent for the synthesis of carbon dots. The polyimine structure of PEI helps in passivating carbon dots with amine group which helps in recognizing analytes with free amine and carboxylic acid group. The representation of the synthesized carbon dots is shown in graphical abstract. The amine branches in the carbon dots will undergo bioconjugation with –COOH group of antibody thereby providing effective immobilization. To the best of our knowledge, the use of MoS2/f-CuCD combination as a electrode modification material in electrochemical immunosensor for HPV-16L1 detection have been addressed for the first time.

In this study, f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE was utilized for the determination of cancer biomarker HPV-16L1. MoS2 nanosheets modify the electrode surface by providing high electrical conductivity and large surface area on which f-CuCD was dispersed. f-CuCD helps in further increasing the conductivity and also ensures effective non-specific bioconjugation of antibody and antigen. The electrochemical signal was recorded using EIS spectroscopy. Thus, this work adopt a label-free impedimetric technique using amine functionalization for sensitive detection of HPV-16L1.

HPV-16L1 and anti-HPV-16L1 were purchased from Abcam, UK. Ammonium molybdate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), the precursor for MoS2 was purchased from SRL, India. polyethlene imine (precursor of carbon dots), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and human serum were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The copper source CuSO4·5H2O was obtained from Finar, India. The constituents of the phosphate buffer solution including monosodium dihydrogen phosphate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, sodium hydroxide were purchased from Himedia, India. Potassium ferricyanide and potassium ferrocyanide were obtained from Loba chemie, India. The electrolytes for the electrochemical experiments were prepared using Milli Q water (∼18 MΩ cm).

The electrochemical experiments were carried out in CHI608D potentiostat from CH instruments. SPCE containing carbon working and counter electrode and an external Ag/AgCl reference electrode was used in this study. XRD data was obtained using Malvern Panalytical Aeris XRD system. The Raman spectrum was obtained using the Renishaw inVia Raman microscope. UV–visible spectroscopy was carried out using Shimadzu UV-1700 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with the frequency limit of 1 MHz-0.1 Hz at an open circuit potential. The results are obtained in the form of a Nyquist plot with real impedance (Z’) on the X-axis and imaginary impedance (Z”) on the Y-axis. The circuit fitting was done using Zsimpwin software with the equivalent circuit [Rs(Qdl(RCTW))] having solution resistance, Rs, constant phase element, Qdl, Warburg element, W and charge transfer resistance, RCT. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and EDAX were undergone using Zeiss Gemini-300. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were analysed using JEOL-2100.

MoS2 nanoflowers were synthesized by a hydrothermal method reported already [35]. Briefly, 500 mg of ammonium molybdate was dissolved in 40 mL of distilled water and then 1 g of thiourea was added and dissolved. To maintain the pH, 500 mg of oxalic acid was added to the clear solution with constant stirring and then the solution was subjected to hydrothermal treatment on a teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) at 200 °C for 24 h. The obtained solution was washed with water and ethanol and centrifuged at 3500 rpm. The black precipitate obtained was allowed to dry in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu doped amine functionalized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of polyethylene imine (PEI) precursor was dissolved in 30 ml of D.I. water with stirring in a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. 0.015 g of CuSO4·5H2O was then added with constant stirring for 1 h followed by sonication for 10–15 min to ensure complete dispersion. The PEI-Cu complex was prepared by mixing PEI and CuSO4.5H20 in the molar ratio of 0.1. After sonication, hydrothermal treatment was carried out on a teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the solution was filtered using syringe filtration to get f-CuCD [36].

SPCE with conductive carbon working and counter electrodes was printed on polyethylene terephthalate substrate (PET), as reported in our previous work [37] with an external Ag/AgCl reference electrode. 2 µL from 1 mg/1 mL solution of MoS2 was utilized to modify the working electrode based on the previous literature report [38,39]. 2 µL from the stock concentration of f-CuCD was then drop casted on the MoS2 surface which is expected to aid the conductivity. Then, 2 µL of anti-HPV was immobilized. The effective immobilization is attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the amine group of the carbon dots and the carboxylic acid group of the antibody.

The crystalline nature of the synthesized MoS2 was detected using XRD as seen from Fig. 1 A. The 2θ peaks at 14.11°, 33.34°, 39.26°, 49.38°, 59.04° and 69.43° correspond to the planes (002), (100), (103), (105), (110) and (201) respectively. These data indicate pure hexagonal phase of MoS2 (2H-MoS2) which is in accordance with the JCPDS card no 37–1492. Bragg's equation (nλ=2d sin θ) was used to calculate the value of d-spacing and it was found to be 0.38 nm.

Fig. 1B represents the Raman spectra of 2H-MoS2. The spectrum shows peaks at 384 cm-1 and 407 cm-1 denoting E1 2g and A1g respectively. E1 2 g peak is due to the in-plane bending vibration where Mo vibrates in a direction opposite to two S atoms. A1g peak is due to out-of-plane bending where only S atoms vibrate. The peak near 450 cm−1 is due to laser acoustic vibrations [40].

Fig. 1C shows the FTIR spectra of MoS2 nanoflowers. The broad peak at 3373 cm-1 represent OH stretching vibration. CO and –COH groups are represented by the peaks at 1622 cm-1 and 1155 cm-1 respectively [41]. The sharp peak at 897 cm-1 denotes S-S stretching vibration mode. The Mo-O and Mo-S vibrations of MoS2 is shown by the peaks at 748 cm-1 and 445 cm-1. The peaks at 1033 cm-1 and 589 cm-1 corresponds to CO and S2 2- vibration [42,43].

The morphological analysis of synthesized MoS2 was carried out using SEM and TEM. Fig. 2 A and B indicate the SEM images of MoS2. The image shows an assembly of flower-like MoS2 nanostructures formed by the combination of MoS2 nanosheets. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) analysis shows the presence of molybdenum (Mo) and sulphur (S) atoms without any impurities as depicted in Fig. S1. TEM image in Fig. 2C shows the formation of thin MoS2 nanosheets in the edges. This confirms the formation of MoS2 nanoflowers through assembly of thin layers of MoS2 nanosheets. The average size of nanoflower-like structure measures approximately 300 nm. Fig. 2D depicts the selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern showing diffraction peaks at (002), (100), (103) and (110) ensuring the polycrystalline nature of MoS2 which is in aggrement with the XRD pattern.

Fig. 3 A shows the UV–Visible spectrum of f-CuCD. The absorption peak at 275 nm shows the presence of sp2 hybridized CC bond due to the π-π* transition [44]. The crystalline structure of f-CuCD was determined using XRD. A broad 2θ diffraction peak was observed at 18.8° (Fig. S2) corresponding to the single crystal graphite phase or turbostratic carbon phase. [33]The sharp peak at 33.3° was due to the silica substrate. The FTIR spectrum of f-CuCD is depicted in Fig. 3B. The broad peaks obtained at 3000–2900 cm-1 and 2950–2800 cm-1 represent NH stretching and CH vibrations respectively. The peak at 1474 cm-1 represents the CN stretching vibration. Also, the strong peaks at 1565 cm-1 and 1110 cm-1 indicate CC and Cu-N stretching vibrations respectively.

The morphological analysis of f-CuCD and MoS2/f-CuCD were analysed using TEM analysis. Fig. 4 A represent the dispersion of f-CuCD and the carbon dots show an average size of 6 nm. The FE-TEM image of MoS2/f-CuCD is represented in Fig. 4 (B,C). The image shows a uniform dispersion of f-CuCD on MoS2 nanosheets and hence the surface shows efficiency towards antibody binding. The size of the carbon dots dispersed on MoS2 nanosheets varies between 4 and 7 nm.

The electrochemical characteristics of MoS2 and f-CuCD were analysed by performing CV and EIS studies. The CV study was recorded in the potential window of −0.2 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in the presence of K3/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing PBS. The addition of MoS2 on the bare SPCE surface resulted in an increase in anodic peak current (IpA) to 5.349 µA at an anodic peak potential (EpA) of 0.3164 V. This increase is attributed to the highly conductive nature of MoS2. A further increase of IpA to 10.8235 µA was observed after the incorporation of f-CuCD on the MoS2/SPCE surface. This is due to the conductive property of copper and effective electrocatalytic activity of the carbon dots. The unmodified and modified SPCE surfaces were subjected to EIS analysis. The semicircular portion indicates the kinetically controlled reactions and the low-frequency linear portion indicates the diffusion controlled process. The semicircular diameter gives the charge transfer resistance (Rct) [45]. The MoS2-modified electrode surface showed a lower Rct of 6.484 × 104 Ω compared to the bare electrode (Rct1.145 × 105 Ω) indicating enhanced conductivity. After the addition of f-CuCD to SPCE/MoS2 surface, the Rct further decreased drastically to 3280 Ω. Then, the electrochemically characterized SPCE/f-CuCD/MoS2 was subjected to antibody-antigen interaction. The anti-HPV was incubated on the fabricated electrode surface for 1 h. The amine group of functionalized CD undergoes bioconjugation with the carboxyl group of anti-HPV. The decreased peak current and increased Rct of 3.693 × 104 Ω as inferred from Fig. 5 (A,B) respectively indicate the successful immobilization of antibody on the sensor surface. Addition of BSA helps in avoiding non-specific interactions by blocking the antibody un-bound regions of the sensor surface. Finally, HPV-16L1 was drop casted on the modified electrode and incubated for 1 h. This resulted in further decrease in peak current and increase in Rct to 1.643 × 105 Ω as shown in Fig. 5 (A,B) respectively confirming the effective antibody-antigen binding. Antibody and antigen are biomolecules lacking conductive properties, due to which they show an increase in Rct and a decrease in peak current.

The analytical performance of the developed immunosensor surface towards different concentrations of HPV-16L1 is shown in Fig. 6 A and B. The EIS response of SPCE/anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2 surface was recorded as a function of HPV-16L1 concentration ranging from 0.01 to 100 ng/µL. The Rct increased with an increase in HPV-16L1 concentration indicating the effective interaction of the immobilized antibody with the antigen surface indicating the formation of immune-complex on the fabricated sensor surface. The corresponding CV behaviour of decreasing peak current with increasing concentration of antigen is shown in Fig. S3(A). As shown in Fig. 6B, there is a linear relation between the Rct and the logarithm of HPV-16L1 concentration with a linear regression equation ΔRct=152,960.51 log C + 454,537.75 having a regression coefficient of 0.9889. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 8.30 pg/µL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of MoS2/SPCE and f-CuCD/SPCE towards HPV-16L1
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviour of MoS2/SPCE and f-CuCD/SPCE surfaces was analyzed towards HPV-16L1 detection. The electrochemical response of SPCE/MoS2 electrode towards anti-HPV (Rct 1.958 × 105 Ω) and HPV-16L1 (Rct 2.288 × 105Ω) binding showed a little subsequent increase in Rct as shown in Fig. 7 A and a small decrease of peak current in CV (Fig. S4(A)). This small increase is because of the affinity of MoS2 surface having sulphur vacancies for antibody fragments which enhances the binding of antigen through chemisorption [46]. Also, EIS and CV response of f-CuCD modified electrode surface towards anti-HPV (Rct 835.6 Ω) and HPV-16L1 (Rct 2335 Ω) showed only a mild increase in Rct (Fig. 7B) with a small decrease in peak current (Fig. S4(B)). Hence, it is shown that SPCE/MoS2 and SPCE/f-CuCD exhibit only a minimal binding of antibody and antigen when compared to SPCE/f-CuCD/MoS2.

The selectivity of the immunosensor towards HPV-16L1 antigen was studied using Immunoglobulin (IgG), HER-2 and CA-125 as interfering antigens. Equal volume of 1 ng/µL concentration of the interferents and HPV-16L1 antigen were mixed together. The EIS response of the interfering antigens is depicted in Fig. S5 (A-D). The percentage change in Rct for HPV-16L1 is high as shown in Fig. 8 A when compared to other antigens thereby confirming the excellent selectivity of the developed immunosensor towards HPV-16L1.

For reporting the reproducibility of the proposed immunosensor, five different electrodes with the same experimental conditions were investigated. The EIS response of each electrode is given in Fig. S6(A). Fig. 8B shows the comparison of Rct between the electrodes. The RSD (relative standard deviation) was found to be <6 % among five electrodes suggesting the excellent reproducibility of the developed immunosensor.

For evaluating the storage stability of the fabricated sensor, the MoS2/f-CuCD/Anti-HPV-16L1 modified electrodes were stored at 4 °C in PBS solution and the CV current was measured for 5 consecutive days. The negligible change in peak current is evident from the bar graph and CV as shown in Fig. 8 (C,D) indicating the stability of the fabricated sensor. Similarly, the stability of the fabricated surface in the absence of antibodies was also tested. CV response was recorded for five consecutive days and the corresponding bar graph also shows negligible difference in peak current as (Fig. S7 (A,B)). This confirms the appreciable stability of f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE and anti-HPV/f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE surface.

The following table lists the comparison of the proposed immunosensor with other reported sensors that adopted electrochemical techniques for determining HPV-16L1. The comparative study of the constructed sensor is found to be in agreement with the reported sensors (Table 1 ).

Herein, we have developed a disposable label-free electrochemical immunosensor using f-CuCD/MoS2/SPCE for the sensitive determination of HPV-16L1. The 2D MoS2 nanosheets help in increasing the electrocatalytic activity of the SPCE surface. The use of highly functionalised carbon dots helped in enhancing the conductivity of the modified surface and immobilizing anti-HPV. The developed sensor exhibited a wide linearity of 0.01–100 ng/µL with a LOD of 8.30 pg/µL. The label-free technique utilized ensures simple and real time analysis of HPV-16L1. The constructed immunosensor showed excellent reproducibility with RSD less than 6 %. The modified electrode surface showed stability until 5 days of storage at 4 °C. The sensor also exhibited a selective response for HPV-16L1 antigen in the presence of interferents. The analysis using spiked serum samples showed an excellent recovery percentage of 91–98 %. Thus, the fabricated immunosensor finds potential application in clinical diagnosis and can also be used for the detection of other antigens by appropriate modification with corresponding antibodies.

Rajmohan Swetha: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Karutha Pandian Divya: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Lakshmi Devi A: Resources. T.G. Satheesh Babu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Biotechnology and Department of Science and Technology, Government of India for financial support (Sanction no. 102/IFD/SAN/1591/2014-15 dated 22-07-2014) and (Project No. SP/YO/2021/2427 (G) dated 25/10/2023). R Swetha thanks Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham for providing financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.snr.2025.100308.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced cervical cancer screening: a sensitive electrochemical approach for HPV 18 L1 protein detection using a gold-graphitic carbon nitride-based immunosensor
J. Electrochem. Soc.
